goal: Compare two repair strategies for a large (~2,000-file) codebase: a minimal <100-line 'instant fix' vs a ~500–1k-line comprehensive fixer, grounded in the provided scripts, with explicit NOC (non-compilation) framing.

notes: In this context, NOC refers to workflows where changes can be applied/validated without a full rebuild (e.g., runtime/JIT, live reload, or targeted test execution).
This deliverable treats NOC as an operational goal: reduce rebuild friction while still maintaining verification gates.

artifacts.name: code_length_comparator
artifacts.purpose: Counts script line lengths via wc/bc (bash).

assumptions.execution_base_dir: Run scripts with the repository root as the single argument.
notes: Full apply means the scripts write changes directly into the repository.
Although 'no setup required' is stated, best practice is to ensure the git working tree is clean and you have a rollback path (branch/commit) before running automation.

under_100_lines_instant_fix.intent: Fast, targeted changes to address a small number of high-impact issues quickly (triage/hotfix style).

how_it_supports_noc: Minimizes time spent in rebuild loops by applying narrowly scoped edits.
Uses a quick test invocation (pytest) instead of full project rebuilds.
limits: Still relies on running tests; not a true runtime hot-reload system.
Coverage is intentionally shallow (samples only a small subset of files).

scope.files_covered: Python only; scans for *.py but then samples only the first 10 files for issue detection.
issue_types_detected: Heuristic: 'import os' present but 'import sys' absent
changes_applied: Insert 'import sys' immediately after an 'import os' line (only when missing).

workflow: Glob for Python files and take a small sample
Identify a short list of issues (top 5)
Apply a narrow fix (sys import insertion)
Run quick pytest with short timeout

tests.coverage_characteristic: Smoke-level; may miss slow or integration tests

outputs: Console summary only (no reports written to disk)
strengths: Very low implementation surface area (78 LOC) => easy to audit and modify
Low time-to-first-result; good for emergency unblocking
Reduced blast radius because it performs very few transformations
weaknesses: Not actually comprehensive for a ~2,000-file project (samples only 10 files)
Issue detection is heuristic and may miss most real defects
Fix logic is brittle (assumes import placement and file structure)

primary_risks: False confidence: passing quick tests may not reflect real health
Misses non-Python sources and most Python files
May introduce stylistic or ordering issues in imports
mitigations_suggested: Expand file coverage from sample to full set before applying changes
Add a dry-run mode and a unified diff output
Gate changes behind a real test suite and CI

expected_time_cost.machine_time: Seconds to a few minutes (depending on pytest discovery)

500_to_1k_lines_comprehensive_fix.intent: Exhaustive analysis plus automated repair steps with backup, reporting, and broader validation (maintenance/refactor style).

how_it_supports_noc: Emphasizes analysis + targeted transformations rather than manual rebuild cycles.
Produces reports/metrics so you can prioritize runtime-friendly fixes (e.g., hotspots) without recompiling everything repeatedly.
Runs verification tools to validate changes without needing full product builds (depending on project structure).
limits: Touches many files and can increase iteration time if test suites are large.
Some transformations are mechanical and may require rebuild-level verification anyway.

scope.files_covered: Multiple languages by extension scan (*.py, *.js, *.ts, *.java, *.cpp, *.c); loads full contents into memory.
analysis_features: Dependency extraction (Python AST imports; JS/TS regex imports)
TODO/FIXME detection across all loaded files
Python cyclomatic complexity heuristic (>10) via AST walk
Regex performance checks (range(len()) style loops, etc.)
Python style checks (line length > 100, trailing whitespace)
changes_applied: TODO resolver inserts placeholder implementation ('pass') after TODO lines (naive)
Breaks long lines with a backslash continuation (naive)
Replaces 'eval(' with 'ast.literal_eval(' and prepends 'import ast' if missing
Regex rewrite for some loop patterns
Adds a 'TODO: Refactor' comment for high-complexity functions (placeholder)

workflow: Create a full backup tree in a temp directory
Apply fixes across all identified issues
Run tests + lint + optional type checks

tests_and_checks.pass_condition: flake8 return code == 0 (logged; not used as global pass condition)

outputs: A complete backup copy of all scanned files under a temp directory
strengths: Wider file coverage and multi-dimensional issue inventory
Produces artifacts (reports) useful for audit and handoff
Runs broader verification steps (tests + lint + optional typing)
weaknesses: Automated fixes are simplistic and can be semantically unsafe (especially TODO insertion and eval replacement)
Loads entire project contents into memory (can be heavy on large repos)
Security/performance detections are regex-based and may produce false positives/negatives
Documentation generator is placeholder (no real docs output)

primary_risks: Semantic breakage from mechanical rewrites (eval->literal_eval, line splitting, loop regex)
Large blast radius: many files can be touched quickly
Test suite coverage may still be insufficient to detect subtle regressions
mitigations_suggested: Add strict dry-run + patch output before writing changes
Restrict fix application to high-confidence transformations
Apply fixes in staged phases with commit checkpoints
Require green CI and run targeted integration tests

expected_time_cost.human_time: Tens of minutes to hours (review + iteration)
expected_time_cost.machine_time: Minutes to tens of minutes (scan + tests), depending on repo size and tooling

dimensions.instant_fix: Low (samples first 10 Python files for issue detection)
dimensions.comprehensive_fix: High (scans many files across multiple languages)
Large potential change set; higher regression risk
Systematic cleanup with review and rollback

choose_instant_fix_if: You need a quick, minimal change to unblock work
You have a narrow known issue class and can accept partial coverage
choose_comprehensive_fix_if: You need a broad inventory of issues and metrics
You can budget time for review and rollback
You want backups and written reports as deliverables
avoid_both_if: The project requires domain-aware fixes (business logic, protocol correctness) where automated heuristics are unsafe without strong guardrails

non_architectural_engineer_guidance.purpose: Use script insights to guide where to spend effort, without prematurely re-architecting.
playbook: Start with metrics (issue counts by type, hotspots by file/function).
Pick the smallest safe automation first (whitespace/import fixes) to reduce noise.
Use the slow script's reports to choose 3–5 high-impact targets (tests failing, security flags, extreme complexity).
Only after stabilizing, consider architectural changes (module boundaries, dependency inversion) informed by dependency graph findings.

preflight_checks: Ensure git working tree is clean
Create a rollback point (commit or branch)
Confirm pytest/flake8 are available if you want verification to run
